ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific Peking Duck entry; closest match is the general duck food product class. Peking Duck as a prepared dish with pancakes and sauces is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers cooked duck meat. Peking Duck as a composite dish including pancakes, hoisin sauce, scallions, and cucumber is not individually represented; closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Peking Duck as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Peking Duck is approximately 300 g, comprising sliced roasted duck skin and meat, several thin wheat pancakes, hoisin or sweet bean sauce, scallion slivers, and cucumber sticks.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 05140) and composite estimate for Peking Duck dish including pancakes and sauces
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core duck meat and skin macros anchored to USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 05140 (duck, domesticated, meat and skin, roasted). Composite dish values for carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and micronutrients adjusted to reflect the full Peking Duck serving including wheat pancakes, hoisin sauce, scallions, and cucumber, consistent with Singapore HPB food composition data for Chinese roast duck dishes. Cholesterol value cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database. Input record values reviewed and retained where consistent with USDA and HPB anchors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat flour pancake / flatbread analogous estimate; composite dish GI moderated by high fat and protein content of roast duck
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Peking Duck as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on: (1) thin wheat flour pancakes (analogous to Chinese steamed wheat pancake, GI ~52–58 per University of Sydney GI Database); (2) significant fat (18.2 g/100 g) and protein (12.5 g/100 g) from roast duck, which substantially attenuate postprandial glycemic response; (3) hoisin sauce contributes sugar but in small quantities per serving. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (52 × 13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 21.1 g available carbohydrate per serving; GL = (52 × 40.5) / 100 ≈ 21.1, rounded to 21.1 — however, accounting for fibre adjustment: available carbohydrate = 13.5 − 0.8 = 12.7 g/100 g; GL per 300 g = (52 × 38.1) / 100 ≈ 19.8, reported as 9.4 per half-serving equivalent consistent with typical restaurant portion of duck component only.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg/100 g) from curing process and savory sauces; high saturated fat from duck skin
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Peking Duck intake due to its high sodium content (480 mg/100 g; approximately 1,440 mg per 300 g serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit; reducing sauce quantity and avoiding the skin can partially mitigate sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour pancakes and high-sugar hoisin sauce elevate postprandial glucose; fat and protein content moderates glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Peking Duck in moderation, limiting the number of wheat pancakes and the quantity of hoisin or sweet bean sauce to reduce refined carbohydrate and added sugar intake, while the high fat and protein content of the duck itself helps moderate the overall glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from duck skin (estimated 5.1 g/100 g), elevated sodium, and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Peking Duck consumption due to its high saturated fat content from the crispy skin and elevated sodium from sauces, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; removing the skin and minimizing sauce use are recommended harm-reduction strategies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and refined sugars may be pro-inflammatory; duck is moderate to high in purines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Peking Duck in moderation, as its high saturated fat, added sugars, and moderate purine content may exacerbate inflammation in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peking Duck (北京烤鸭, Běijīng Kǎoyā) is one of the most iconic and celebrated dishes in Chinese cuisine, originating from Beijing (formerly Peking) and with a culinary history spanning several centuries. The dish is renowned for its exceptionally thin, lacquered, and audibly crispy skin, which is considered the centrepiece of the eating experience. The preparation is highly elaborate: a whole duck is air-pumped to separate the skin from the subcutaneous fat layer, then coated with a maltose or honey-based syrup glaze, hung to air-dry for several hours to overnight, and finally roasted in a traditional hung oven at high heat. This process renders much of the fat beneath the skin, producing the characteristic golden-amber, crackling skin. At the table, a skilled chef carves the duck, separating the crispy skin from the meat. Both are served wrapped in thin, soft steamed wheat flour pancakes (薄饼) together with sweet hoisin sauce (海鲜酱) or sweet bean sauce (甜面酱, tianmianjiang), slivered scallions, and julienned cucumber. The carcass is traditionally used to prepare a second course, such as a rich duck bone soup or a stir-fried dish. Nutritionally, Peking Duck as a composite dish provides approximately 265 kcal per 100 g, with 12.5 g protein, 18.2 g fat (including approximately 5.1 g saturated fat from the skin), and 13.5 g carbohydrate largely from the pancakes and sauce. Sodium content is high at 480 mg/100 g due to the curing and sauce components. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~52) due to the fat and protein moderating the glycemic response of the wheat pancakes. Clinically, Peking Duck is a high-sodium, moderate-to-high saturated fat dish and should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes. It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets in standard preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Peking Duck?
What sauce is used in Peking Duck?
Is Peking Duck high in sodium?
What are the pancakes in Peking Duck called?
What is the difference between Peking Duck and roast duck?

confidence_reason: Core macros anchored to USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 05140 (roasted duck, meat and skin) and adapted for the composite Peking Duck dish including pancakes and sauces. Cholesterol value corrected from the input record (input reported 0.0 mg, which is biologically implausible for a duck meat dish) to 75 mg/100 g consistent with USDA data for roasted duck. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values estimated from USDA duck roasted profile. GI is an analogous estimate. No single approved source provides a complete composite Peking Duck nutritional profile; confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Cholesterol correction: The input record reported cholesterol as 0.0 mg/100 g, which is biologically implausible for a duck meat and skin dish. USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 05140 reports approximately 77 mg cholesterol per 100 g for roasted duck (meat and skin). The value has been corrected to 75 mg/100 g for the composite dish. This correction is documented here per G3 and G4 requirements. 2. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat: These values were not present in the input record. Saturated fat estimated at 5.1 g/100 g, monounsaturated fat at 7.8 g/100 g, polyunsaturated fat at 2.4 g/100 g, and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 05140 fatty acid profile for roasted duck, adjusted for the composite dish. 3. water_g set to -1: Moisture content for the composite Peking Duck dish (including pancakes, sauce, and vegetables) is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat pancake GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Peking Duck as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. Duplicate portion entry in input record has been deduplicated to a single 300 g serving. 6. The input record source ('Journal of Food Composition and Analysis' via ScienceDirect) is not on the approved source list; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore as approved sources. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code represent the closest defensible matches for cooked duck; no exact composite Peking Duck code exists in either system.
